%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Section Objective

Predict frequency response from the locations of poles and zeros of \(H(s)\). Use distances and angles in the s-plane to explain gain peaks, notches, and phase changes.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Pole-zero product form
- Vector factors \(p - z_i\) and \(p - \lambda_i\)
- Magnitude response from distances
- Phase response from angles
- Gain enhancement by a pole
- Gain suppression by a zero

## 1. Pole-zero product form

This form rewrites a transfer function as a product of factors, each tied to a specific zero or pole location in the complex s-plane.

**Symbol definitions:**
- \(z_i\) — zeros: values of \(s\) where the numerator is zero
- \(\lambda_i\) — poles: values of \(s\) where the denominator is zero
- \(b_0\) — constant gain factor (a real scalar)

**When to use it:** Whenever a problem gives a pole-zero plot or asks for qualitative frequency response, rewrite \(H(s)\) in this product form first.

**Minimal example:** A factor \((s - z_1)\) contributes a distance from zero \(z_1\) to the evaluation point, while \((s - \lambda_1)\) contributes a distance from pole \(\lambda_1\) to the evaluation point.

#### Common Misuse
Do not treat poles and zeros as just algebraic roots. Their geometric locations in the s-plane directly control the shape of the frequency response.

$$H(s)=b_0\frac{(s-z_1)(s-z_2)\cdots(s-z_N)}{(s-\lambda_1)(s-\lambda_2)\cdots(s-\lambda_N)}$$

## 2. Evaluating H(s) at a point p

Substituting \(s = p\) turns every pole-zero factor into a **directed vector** in the complex plane.

**Vector definitions:**
- \(p - z_i\): directed vector **from zero \(z_i\) to point \(p\)**
- \(p - \lambda_i\): directed vector **from pole \(\lambda_i\) to point \(p\)**

Each vector has both a **length** (magnitude) and an **angle** (argument measured from the positive real axis).

**Frequency-response trigger:** For \(H(j\omega)\), the evaluation point \(p = j\omega\) moves along the imaginary axis as \(\omega\) varies. This sweeps all the vectors continuously.

**Minimal example:** If \(p\) gets very close to a pole \(\lambda_k\), the denominator vector \(p - \lambda_k\) becomes very short, driving the magnitude of \(H\) upward.

#### Common Misuse
Do not draw vectors **from \(p\) to the root**. The correct direction is from the root to \(p\). Reversing the direction flips the angle by \(\pi\), giving the wrong phase.

$$\left.H(s)\right|_{s=p}=b_0\frac{(p-z_1)(p-z_2)\cdots(p-z_N)}{(p-\lambda_1)(p-\lambda_2)\cdots(p-\lambda_N)}\tag{4.53}$$

## 3. Magnitude response comes from distances

**Distance definitions:**
- \(r_i = |p - z_i|\) — distance from zero \(z_i\) to evaluation point \(p\)
- \(d_i = |p - \lambda_i|\) — distance from pole \(\lambda_i\) to evaluation point \(p\)

In plain English: **magnitude equals constant gain times the product of zero distances divided by the product of pole distances.**

**How each distance affects gain:**
- Large zero distances \(r_i\) increase magnitude; small zero distances suppress it.
- Small pole distances \(d_i\) increase magnitude; large pole distances reduce it.

**Exam trigger:** If a pole or zero lies near the imaginary-axis point \(j\omega_0\), expect a gain peak or dip near \(\omega = \omega_0\).

#### Common Misuse
Poles are in the **denominator**, so a shorter pole distance makes gain **larger**, not smaller. Students often confuse this with zero behavior. This formula assumes positive \(b_0\); a negative \(b_0\) adds a sign flip.

$$\left|H(s)\right|_{s=p}=b_0\frac{r_1 r_2\cdots r_N}{d_1 d_2\cdots d_N}\tag{4.54}$$

## 4. Phase response comes from angles

**Angle definitions:**
- \(\phi_i = \angle(p - z_i)\) — angle of the vector from zero \(z_i\) to \(p\), measured from the positive real axis
- \(\theta_i = \angle(p - \lambda_i)\) — angle of the vector from pole \(\lambda_i\) to \(p\), measured from the positive real axis

**Rule:** Zero angles **add**; pole angles **subtract**.

**When to use it:** Any phase-response problem based on a pole-zero plot.

**Minimal example:** One zero and one pole gives phase \(= \phi_1 - \theta_1\), the zero angle minus the pole angle.

#### Common Misuse
- Do not use distances to compute phase — distances give magnitude only.
- Do not add pole angles; they must be subtracted.

If \(b_0 < 0\), the phase has an additional \(\pi\) radians.

$$\angle\left.H(s)\right|_{s=p}=(\phi_1+\phi_2+\cdots+\phi_N)-(\theta_1+\theta_2+\cdots+\theta_N)\tag{4.55}$$

## 5. Gain enhancement by a pole

For a single pole near \(-\alpha + j\omega_0\), the variable \(d\) is the distance from that pole to the moving point \(j\omega\) on the imaginary axis, and \(K\) is a constant scale factor.

**How the peak forms:** As \(\omega \to \omega_0\), the point \(j\omega\) comes closest to the pole, so \(d\) becomes small and \(K/d\) becomes large — producing a resonance or gain peak near \(\omega_0\).

**Exam pattern:** A pole near \(j\omega_0\) causes a gain peak near \(\omega = \omega_0\). The closer the pole to the imaginary axis, the taller and narrower the peak.

**Minimal example:** Reducing \(\alpha\) moves the pole closer to the imaginary axis, sharpening the peak. Setting \(\alpha = 0\) places the pole on the imaginary axis.

#### Stability Warning
For a stable continuous-time system, the pole must remain in the **left half-plane** (\(\alpha > 0\)). Placing a pole exactly on the imaginary axis violates stability and is not valid in a stable filter design.

$$|H(j\omega)|=\frac{K}{d}\tag{4.56}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Product form: \(H(s)=b_0\dfrac{\prod_i(s-z_i)}{\prod_i(s-\lambda_i)}\) — zeros in numerator, poles in denominator.
- Eq. (4.53): substituting \(s=p\) turns each factor into a directed vector from root to \(p\).
- Eq. (4.54): \(|H(p)|=b_0\dfrac{r_1 r_2\cdots}{d_1 d_2\cdots}\) — magnitude is ratio of zero distances to pole distances.
- Eq. (4.55): \(\angle H(p)=\sum\phi_i - \sum\theta_i\) — zero angles add, pole angles subtract.
- Eq. (4.56): \(|H(j\omega)|=K/d\) — pole near \(j\omega_0\) sharpens gain peak; zero on imaginary axis gives zero gain; stable poles must stay in the left half-plane.

- Core formula: \(H(s)=b_0\frac{(s-z_1)(s-z_2)\cdots(s-z_N)}{(s-\lambda_1)(s-\lambda_2)\cdots(s-\lambda_N)}\).
- Core formula: \(\left.H(s)\right|_{s=p}=b_0\frac{(p-z_1)(p-z_2)\cdots(p-z_N)}{(p-\lambda_1)(p-\lambda_2)\cdots(p-\lambda_N)}\tag{4.53}\).
- Core formula: \(\left|H(s)\right|_{s=p}=b_0\frac{r_1 r_2\cdots r_N}{d_1 d_2\cdots d_N}\tag{4.54}\).
- Core formula: \(\angle\left.H(s)\right|_{s=p}=(\phi_1+\phi_2+\cdots+\phi_N)-(\theta_1+\theta_2+\cdots+\theta_N)\tag{4.55}\).
*In the next section we will use these pole-zero placement ideas to build simple filter types.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
